content.thinking: Let me start by studying the existing codebase patterns to understand how components are built in this shadcn/ui library.

content.text: I'll start by studying the existing component patterns and registry files to understand the conventions.

input.prompt:
I need to understand the patterns used in the shadcn/ui component library. Please read and return the full contents of these files:

1. `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/carousel.tsx` — a compound component example
2. `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/tabs.tsx` — another compound component
3. `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/accordion.tsx` — another compound component
4. `apps/v4/lib/utils.ts` — the cn() utility
5. `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/_registry.ts` — the UI registry (just the first ~80 lines and last ~20 lines to see the pattern)
6. `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/examples/_registry.ts` — the examples registry (first ~80 lines and last ~20 lines)

Also look at one example file to see the demo pattern:
7. `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/examples/carousel-demo.tsx` or similar demo file
8. `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/examples/tabs-demo.tsx` or similar

Report the full contents of each file so I can understand the exact conventions used.

Also look at one example file to see the demo pattern:

Report the full contents of each file so I can understand the exact conventions used.

1 "use client" 2 3 import * as React from "react" 4 import useEmblaCarousel, { 5 type UseEmblaCarouselType, 6 } from "embla-carousel-react" 7 import { ArrowLeft, ArrowRight } from "lucide-react" 8 9 import { cn } from "@/lib/utils" 10 import { Button } from "@/registry/new-york-v4/ui/button" 11 12 type CarouselApi = UseEmblaCarouselType[1] 13 type UseCarouselParameters = Parameters 14 type CarouselOptions = UseCarouselParameters[0] 15 type CarouselPlugin = UseCarouselParameters[1] 16 17 type CarouselProps = { 18 opts?: CarouselOptions 19 plugins?: CarouselPlugin 20 orientation?: "horizontal" | "vertical" 21 setApi?: (api: CarouselApi) => void 22 } 23 24 type CarouselContextProps = { 25 carouselRef: ReturnType[0] 26 api: ReturnType[1] 27 scrollPrev: () => void 28 scrollNext: () => void 29 canScrollPrev: boolean 30 canScrollNext: boolean 31 } & CarouselProps 32 33 const CarouselContext = React.createContext(null) 34 35 function useCarousel() { 36 const context = React.useContext(CarouselContext) 37 38 if (!context) { 39 throw new Error("useCarousel must be used within a ") 40 } 41 42 return context 43 } 44 45 function Carousel({ 46 orientation = "horizontal", 47 opts, 48 setApi, 49 plugins, 50 className, 51 children, 52 ...props 53 }: React.ComponentProps<"div"> & CarouselProps) { 54 const [carouselRef, api] = useEmblaCarousel( 55 { 56 ...opts, 57 axis: orientation === "horizontal" ? "x" : "y", 58 }, 59 plugins 60 ) 61 const [canScrollPrev, setCanScrollPrev] = React.useState(false) 62 const [canScrollNext, setCanScrollNext] = React.useState(false) 63 64 const onSelect = React.useCallback((api: CarouselApi) => { 65 if (!api) return 66 setCanScrollPrev(api.canScrollPrev()) 67 setCanScrollNext(api.canScrollNext()) 68 }, []) 69 70 const scrollPrev = React.useCallback(() => { 71 api?.scrollPrev() 72 }, [api]) 73 74 const scrollNext = React.useCallback(() => { 75 api?.scrollNext() 76 }, [api]) 77 78 const handleKeyDown = React.useCallback( 79 (event: React.KeyboardEvent) => { 80 if (event.key === "ArrowLeft") { 81 event.preventDefault() 82 scrollPrev() 83 } else if (event.key === "ArrowRight") { 84 event.preventDefault() 85 scrollNext() 86 } 87 }, 88 [scrollPrev, scrollNext] 89 ) 90 91 React.useEffect(() => { 92 if (!api || !setApi) return 93 setApi(api) 94 }, [api, setApi]) 95 96 React.useEffect(() => { 97 if (!api) return 98 onSelect(api) 99 api.on("reInit", onSelect) 100 api.on("select", onSelect) 101 102 return () => { 103 api?.off("select", onSelect) 104 } 105 }, [api, onSelect]) 106 107 return ( 108 121

content.thinking:
Wait, using two `<Stepper>` components is a bit awkward. A cleaner approach would be to make the Stepper component render a wrapper that doesn't force flex layout on content sections. Let me reconsider the approach.

Actually, looking at the requirements more carefully:
- `Stepper` — root container with flex layout
- `StepperContent` — content panel that reads from context

The idiomatic way in a stepper component would be to have the Stepper wrap both the step indicators and the content panels. The stepper nav and content area are different concerns but should be in the same context.

But the issue is that `Stepper` renders a flex row (horizontal) or flex col (vertical) div, and having content panels mixed in with step items would mess up the layout.


I keep circling back to the same problem — the Stepper's flex layout conflicts with how content panels should be positioned. Actually, I think the two-Stepper approach might be the pragmatic solution here. Both instances can share the same `activeStep` state through context, so the first one handles the step indicators while the second manages the content panels.

The real issue is that having two separate Stepper divs with flex-row layout means only one content panel shows at a time anyway since the others return null, so it's actually fine. But from an API design perspective, it still feels awkward. The cleaner pattern would be a single Stepper that wraps both the indicators and content area without forcing a specific layout on its immediate children. I'm going back and forth on the structure, but I think the cleanest solution is to put everything inside a single Stepper component and use internal layout divs to separate the navigation row from the content below, rather than nesting multiple Steppers. I'm going in circles here—let me step back and look at how other UI libraries structure steppers. The standard pattern is that the Stepper wrapper provides context, and then individual Step components handle their own layout and rendering.

For the actual implementation, I'll keep the Stepper using `flex-col` as the overall container, then nest the step indicators in a separate row-based layout for horizontal orientation. This way the content panels naturally stack below while the indicators stay aligned horizontally. the second Stepper with flex-row styling would constrain the content panel awkwardly. Let me restructure this instead—I'll make the Stepper render as flex-col with a data-orientation attribute, and let child components respond to that attribute via selectors rather than relying on the root flex direction. This mirrors how the Tabs component handles orientation.

Writing component structure... I'm realizing the core issue: if Stepper is flex-col, the step items and separators would each take their own row, which breaks the horizontal layout where they should flow together. I think the cleanest solution is to add a StepperList wrapper like Tabs does, so the nav items can be grouped and styled independently from the content panels below. I need to reconsider the architecture here. The StepperContent components need to use context to know the activeStep, but that means they have to live inside the Stepper wrapper. I'll make the Stepper flexible enough to handle both the step indicators and the content panels, keeping the flex layout only on the indicators section while letting the content render separately below. I'm reconsidering the structure—what if the Stepper just acts as a simple flex container with the provider, and I let the step items arrange themselves naturally? Looking at how the demo would be organized, I'd have the step indicators and separators grouped in their own row, then the content panels stacked below.

The key insight is that `flex flex-col` with a gap is actually the right approach for the root Stepper—it handles the overall vertical layout, while the orientation prop gets passed through context to inform how children should behave, not necessarily forcing the root's flex direction to change. I'm realizing StepperContent needs access to the active step state, so I'll make it accept `activeStep` as a direct prop when context isn't available, keeping the API simple without requiring an extra provider wrapper.

For the layout, I'll structure the Stepper as a context provider that handles the flex direction based on orientation, then position StepperContent below the step indicators using CSS rather than trying to manage everything through flexbox alone. I'm settling on using `flex-wrap` with `basis-full` for the StepperContent so it breaks to a new line in horizontal mode, while vertical orientation just stacks everything in a column. Let me finalize the Stepper component implementation with explicit flex-row and flex-wrap classes for horizontal orientation.

content.text: Wait, I realize there's a layout issue. The Stepper is `flex-row` for horizontal mode, but StepperContent panels need to appear below the step indicators. Let me fix both the component and demo to work properly together. I'll use `flex-wrap` so content panels can break to a new line:

apps/v4/tsconfig.json
{
  "name": "v4",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "pnpm registry:build && pnpm icons:dev & next dev --turbopack --port 4000",
    "build": "pnpm registry:build && next build",
    "start": "next start --port 4000",
    "preview": "pnpm registry:build && next build && next start --port 4000",
    "lint": "eslint . --cache --cache-location .eslintcache",
    "lint:fix": "eslint --fix . --cache --cache-location .eslintcache",
    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit",
    "format:write": "prettier --write \"**/*.{ts,tsx,mdx}\" --cache",
    "format:check": "prettier --check \"**/*.{ts,tsx,mdx}\" --cache",
    "icons:dev": "tsx --tsconfig ./tsconfig.scripts.json ./scripts/build-icons.ts --watch",
    "registry:build": "pnpm --filter=shadcn build && bun run ./scripts/build-registry.mts",
    "registry:capture": "tsx --tsconfig ./tsconfig.scripts.json ./scripts/capture-registry.mts",
    "explore:capture": "tsx --tsconfig ./tsconfig.scripts.json ./scripts/capture-explore.mts",

content.text: Dependencies aren't installed (which is expected in this environment). The code is syntactically and structurally correct based on the patterns I studied. Let me do a final review of everything that was created/modified: **Summary of all changes:** ### 1. New file: `apps/v4/registry/new-york-v4/ui/stepper.tsx` A compound component with 7 exports, following all shadcn/ui conventions: - **`Stepper`** — Root context provider + flex container with `data-slot="stepper"`, `data-orientation`, supports `activeStep` and `orientation` props. Uses `flex-wrap` for horizontal layout so StepperContent can break to a new line below the step indicators. - **`StepperItem`** — Step wrapper with `data-slot="stepper-item"`, `data-step`, and `data-state` (`"completed"` | `"active"` | `"pending"`) derived from context. Provides its own context for child components. - **`StepperTrigger`** — Clickable `
